%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Analog and Digital Systems

> **Section Objective:** Learn to classify systems as analog or digital based on the types of signals at their input and output.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Analog system
- Digital system
- Digital computer
- Digital vs discrete-time

## 1. Analog System

An analog system is defined entirely by its signal types: both the input and the output must be analog signals.

In the notation above, \(x(t)\) is the input signal, \(y(t)\) is the output signal, \(t\) represents continuous time, and \(\mathcal{T}\) represents the system transformation.

**When to use this:** Apply this label whenever a problem describes continuously varying physical signals — such as voltage, sound pressure, or temperature — at both the input and output.

**Minimal example:** An audio amplifier that receives an analog voltage from a microphone and delivers an analog voltage to a speaker is an analog system.

#### Common Misuse

Do not classify a system as analog because it is electronic or physical. Classification depends strictly on whether the input and output signals vary continuously — not on the technology used to build the system.

$$\text{Analog system:}\quad x(t)\ \xrightarrow{\ \mathcal{T}\ }\ y(t)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="web_reference_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Pulse-width modulation](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/PWM%2C_3-level.svg)
*Reference visual from Wikipedia: Pulse-width modulation*
%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-reference-source"><a href="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/PWM%2C_3-level.svg" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Reference image from Wikipedia</a></div>%%KC_END%%


## 2. Digital System

A digital system has digital signals at both its input and its output.

The notation \(x[n]\) and \(y[n]\) uses the index \(n\) to mark discrete sample positions rather than continuous time. Crucially, digital signals do not just have discrete time — their amplitude values are also restricted to a discrete set of allowed levels, most commonly binary levels.

**Exam trigger:** If a problem describes both input and output as bit streams, digital samples, binary data, or sequences of digital codes, classify the system as digital.

**Minimal example:** A calculator that receives key presses as digital codes and produces digital display data is a digital system.

#### Trap

A system whose output is digital but whose input is analog does not qualify as a digital system under this definition. Both sides must be digital.

$$\text{Digital system:}\quad x[n]\ \xrightarrow{\ \mathcal{T}\ }\ y[n]$$

## 3. Binary Digital Computer

A digital computer is a digital system. In the common binary case, its signal alphabet is \(\mathcal{A}=\{0,1\}\).

\(\mathcal{A}\) denotes the set of allowed signal values. The symbols \(0\) and \(1\) are the only levels used to encode all information processed by the computer.

**When to use this:** Apply this formula when a problem mentions binary signals, bits, logic levels, or computer data.

**Minimal example:** A memory cell storing one bit holds a value of either \(0\) or \(1\) — no other value is permitted.

#### Common Misuse

Binary does not mean the system can carry only two total messages. Long strings of \(0\)s and \(1\)s can represent an enormous number of distinct messages. The alphabet has two symbols; the message space is much larger.

$$\mathcal{A}=\{0,1\}$$

## 4. Digital vs Discrete-Time

The textbook notes that a digital computer is both a digital system and a discrete-time system. The implication arrow above captures this: because digital signals are processed at discrete sample or clock positions, every digital system is also a discrete-time system.

**Exam warning — do not reverse the arrow.** A discrete-time signal can still have continuously valued amplitudes. Such a signal is discrete-time but not digital, because its values are not restricted to an allowed alphabet.

**Near-miss example:** A sampled temperature sequence recorded at integer times \(n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\) with real-valued amplitudes is discrete-time. It is not necessarily digital unless those amplitudes are quantized into a finite set of allowed levels.

#### Quick Check

Ask two questions: (1) Are the time positions indexed discretely? (2) Are the amplitude values restricted to an allowed set of levels? Both must be yes for the signal to be digital.

$$\text{digital}\Rightarrow\text{discrete-time}$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Analog system: both input and output are analog signals — \(x(t)\xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}}y(t)\).
- Digital system: both input and output are digital signals — \(x[n]\xrightarrow{\mathcal{T}}y[n]\).
- Binary digital computer uses alphabet \(\mathcal{A}=\{0,1\}\); long bit strings encode many messages.
- \(\text{digital}\Rightarrow\text{discrete-time}\), but discrete-time does not automatically mean digital.

*Next, use these labels whenever a problem asks you to classify a system from its input and output signals.*
